incorrects.description: Student selected 'They are all odd numbers'

params.description: Even number examples highlight: 2, 4, 6. Odd number examples (not present) dim or remain unhighlighted.

steps.text: Look at 2, 4, and 6. Those numbers split perfectly into groups of 2 with no leftovers. That's what makes them even, not odd.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'They are all greater than 10'

params.description: Products less than or equal to 10 highlight: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10.

steps.text: Check the left side of the strip. 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 are all less than or equal to 10. What pattern works for ALL the numbers?

params.description: Ones digit of each product highlights: 2, 4, 6, 8, 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 0.

steps.text: Look at the ones digit of each product. None of them end in 5. What do you notice about the ones digits you see?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Each product on the strip pairs up into groups of 2 with no remainder, demonstrating the even property visually.

steps.text: Let me show you. Every one of these numbers splits perfectly into groups of 2. That's what makes a number even. 2 makes a group, 4 makes two groups, 6 makes three groups. All the products of 2 are even numbers. That's the pattern: multiply by 2, the answer is always even.
